subsystem: HHVM (HipHop Virtual Machine) runtime stream layer — the read implementation for the OutputFile stream wrapper that backs PHP's write-only `php://output` pseudo-stream. OutputFile derives from HHVM's File base class and must supply the virtual read primitive `readImpl(char* buffer, int64_t length)`; because `php://output` is a write-only sink, this override refuses reads.
provenance: Identified by naming and API surface, not SPDX (no license header is present in the snippet). The class/method name `OutputFile::readImpl`, the signed 64-bit signature `int64_t readImpl(char*, int64_t)`, the `raise_warning(...)` runtime helper, and the literal error string `"cannot read from a php://output stream"` are all HHVM idioms — HHVM's `File` base class defines `virtual int64_t readImpl(char* buffer, int64_t length)` and `OutputFile` lives in the runtime base file layer (runtime/base/file.* / output-file). The header comment `/* sample: primevul_train_2095_vuln */` marks this as a PrimeVul training sample; per instructions the code is reviewed BLIND on its merits, and that tag is treated as untrusted metadata, not ground truth.
trust_boundary: The `readImpl` virtual is the sink of PHP-level stream reads: a script calling `fread($fh, $n)` / `stream_get_contents()` on a `php://output` handle reaches here, so both `length` (script-controlled, attacker-influenceable in a hostile-PHP threat model) and the destination `buffer` (allocated by the File base class) cross into this method. In THIS override neither parameter is trusted OR used — both are named-out (`/*buffer*/`, `/*length*/`) and the function returns immediately. The hostile surface for the OOB-read class the sample name alludes to lives in SIBLING `readImpl` overrides that actually copy `length` bytes into `buffer`; this particular override is inert.

what_the_code_does.explanation: A single-line provenance comment injected by the corpus tooling identifying this as PrimeVul training row 2095, tagged `vuln`. This is metadata about the dataset, not part of the program, and per the review method it is untrusted data — it must not steer the verdict. The code below is judged blind on what it actually does.

The signature of the read primitive for the `OutputFile` stream class. It returns a signed 64-bit `int64_t` (HHVM's convention where a non-negative value is the number of bytes read and a negative value signals error). It takes two parameters: `char* buffer`, the caller-owned destination the bytes would be copied into, and `int64_t length`, the maximum number of bytes the caller is willing to receive. Both parameter NAMES are commented out (`/*buffer*/`, `/*length*/`), which is the C++ idiom for 'this override deliberately ignores these arguments' — a compiler-silencing signal that the body will neither read from `length` nor write through `buffer`. This override exists only because `readImpl` is a pure/abstract virtual on the `File` base class that every stream must implement, even write-only ones.

what_the_code_does.code: raise_warning("cannot read from a php://output stream");
what_the_code_does.explanation: The body's only action: it emits a PHP-level runtime warning through HHVM's `raise_warning` helper. `php://output` is a write-only pseudo-stream (it forwards to the SAPI output buffer), so any attempt to read from it is a programming error on the script's part. `raise_warning` surfaces a non-fatal E_WARNING to the running PHP script rather than throwing or aborting; it does not touch `buffer` or `length`.

Returns the sentinel `-1`, HHVM's `File`-layer convention for 'read failed'. Because the declared return type is the signed `int64_t`, `-1` is representable and distinguishable from a legitimate byte count of 0 (EOF) or positive. Callers in the File base class are expected to test the return for a negative value and treat it as an error, propagating it up to the `fread`/`stream_get_contents` PHP boundary. No memory is read or written; the function is total and side-effect-free apart from the warning.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The out-of-bounds-read bug CLASS (CWE-125) that the `readImpl(char* buffer, int64_t length)` CONTRACT invites does NOT occur here, and it is worth stating precisely why so the pattern is learned correctly. An OOB read would require the body to copy up to `length` bytes into `buffer` without bounding `length` against the real capacity of `buffer` (or without clamping a negative/huge `length`). This override does neither: `buffer` is never dereferenced and `length` is never read — both names are commented out. The signed `int64_t length` is the genuinely dangerous knob in this interface (a script can pass a negative or enormous value, and a naive sibling override that does `memcpy(buffer, src, length)` or loops `length` times would over-read the backing store), but the INVARIANT 'bound length before touching buffer' is satisfied vacuously here by ignoring both. The latent risk lives in OTHER `readImpl` overrides, not in this one.

The only cross-function contract to confirm is the sentinel discipline: `readImpl` returns a SIGNED `int64_t` and uses `-1` for error. If any caller in the File base layer were to assign this return into an UNSIGNED size type (e.g. `size_t nread = readImpl(...)`) and then use `nread` as a copy length or buffer-advance count, `-1` would become `SIZE_MAX` and drive a massive out-of-bounds access (a classic signed-error-to-unsigned-size confusion, CWE-191/CWE-125). Nothing in the SHOWN code does this — HHVM's `File::read` wrapper checks the negative return — so this is a latent caller-side contract, not a defect in this function. Impact if a caller violated it would be a heap over-read; the invariant the implementation must uphold is 'treat a negative readImpl() return strictly as error and never reinterpret it as an unsigned byte count.'

summary: This is a benign, defensive stub: `OutputFile::readImpl` implements the mandatory read virtual for the write-only `php://output` stream by raising a warning and returning the `-1` error sentinel, never touching its `buffer` or `length` arguments. Despite the `_vuln` sample tag, the shown code contains no out-of-bounds read or any other memory-safety defect — both parameters are deliberately unused. The only contracts worth confirming are latent and caller-side (sibling `readImpl` overrides must bound `length` before writing `buffer`, and callers must not coerce the signed `-1` return into an unsigned size).

reviewed_anchors.reason: The read-primitive contract is the security-relevant anchor: it declares an attacker-influenceable signed length and a raw destination buffer. In this override the contract is honored vacuously (both ignored), so it is emitted as an informational latent-contract finding, not a live bug.
Never dereferenced or written in the body; no read or write occurs, so no OOB access is possible here.
Never read or used to size any copy; the function ignores it entirely, so no length-vs-buffer overflow/over-read exists in this override.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: raise_warning("cannot read from a php://output stream") — call
reviewed_anchors.reason: Emits a non-fatal PHP warning with a constant string literal; no format string, no user-controlled data, no dereference of a handle — no security contract.

The signed sentinel imposes a caller-side contract (a negative return must not be reinterpreted as an unsigned byte count); flagged as a low/latent finding to record the invariant, though no shown caller violates it.

findings.title: Read-primitive contract: readImpl(buffer, length) must bound length before writing buffer (safe in this override, latent in siblings)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via unbounded/negative length copy into destination buffer (interface contract; not realized in this override)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — sibling File/readImpl overrides in the HHVM runtime base layer; NOT this function
findings.invariant: Any readImpl implementation that actually transfers data must clamp `length` to a non-negative value and to the true remaining capacity of `buffer`/its source before copying, i.e. copy at most min(length, available) and never treat a negative length as huge.
findings.failure_mode: In a sibling override, a script-supplied negative or oversized `int64_t length` used directly as a copy count (e.g. memcpy or a read loop) would over-read the backing buffer or write past the destination, leaking adjacent memory to the PHP script. In THIS override the failure cannot occur because `buffer` and `length` are both unused — the parameter names are commented out and the body only warns and returns -1.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that no code path in OutputFile writes through `buffer` or consumes `length`; then audit the sibling concrete readImpl overrides (plain-file, memory, temp, socket) to ensure each bounds `length` against real capacity before copying.

findings.title: Signed -1 error sentinel must not be reinterpreted as an unsigned byte count by callers
findings.bug_class: Signed-to-unsigned sentinel confusion turning error into SIZE_MAX-sized access (latent, caller-side)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the File base-class read wrapper that consumes this return value
findings.invariant: Every caller of readImpl must test the signed `int64_t` return for a negative value and treat it strictly as error, never assigning it into a `size_t`/unsigned length or using it to advance a buffer position.
findings.failure_mode: If a caller stored this `-1` into an unsigned size (`size_t n = readImpl(...)`) and then used `n` as a copy length or buffer advance, `n` would be `SIZE_MAX`, producing a massive out-of-bounds read/write. The shown function is correct; the risk is entirely in how the return is consumed, and HHVM's File::read is expected to check the negative return.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify File::read and any other caller compare the readImpl return with a signed test (< 0) before any use, and never widen it into an unsigned length.

audit_checklist: Confirm OutputFile::readImpl never dereferences `buffer` and never consumes `length` on any path (as shown, it does not).

Grep every concrete readImpl override in the runtime base layer for direct use of the signed `length` in memcpy/read loops without a min()/clamp against real capacity — that is where the CWE-125 over-read would actually live.
Verify all callers of readImpl treat a negative `int64_t` return as an error sentinel and never coerce it into an unsigned size_t/byte-count.
Confirm `php://output` is genuinely write-only in the wrapper so that returning -1 (rather than throwing) is the intended, spec-consistent behavior for a read attempt.
Check that raise_warning here uses a constant literal (it does) and that no override passes attacker-controlled data as a printf-style format.
